replication_study.hypothesis: Participants assigned to the lottery-ticket frame will report a lower mean willingness-to-pay (WTP) than participants assigned to the gift-certificate frame, when WTP is measured in U.S. dollars after passing the comprehension check (check == 3).

data_plan.source_type: Online survey experiment (Qualtrics, Amazon MTurk recruitment)
data_plan.wave_or_subset: Single data-collection wave that re-implements the original framing experiment
data_plan.sample_size: 281 raw records; expected N ≈ 250 after cleaning (exact N obtained programmatically in replicate_analysis.py)
data_plan.access_details: Openly provided in /app/data via SCORE ‘replication_data’ folder; no additional permission required.

qualification.explanation: The dataset is a fresh sample collected with the same experimental materials and procedure, allowing a direct replication while ensuring statistical independence from the original MTurk samples.
qualification.similarity_to_original:
• Uses the identical framing manipulation (lottery ticket vs gift certificate) and incentive-compatible WTP elicitation described in the original study (post_registration.json → method.description; original_paper.pdf, Experiment 1).
• Employs the same comprehension check logic (correct answer coded as 3) and dependent measure (numeric WTP) as detailed in the shared R code Script.R (replication_data/Script.R).
qualification.deviation_from_original:
• Collects data in 2020 rather than 2011–2012 MTurk cohorts (initial_details.txt vs timestamp embedded in file name ‘22102020’).
• Focuses only on the core 2-level framing factor, whereas the original article contained four experiments and additional factors such as payoff magnitude (post_registration.json → data.wave_or_subset).

data_plan.notes: CSV contains a leading Unicode BOM/garbled first column (‘ï..Id’) and currency strings (e.g., “$20”). replicate_analysis.py cleans these issues; all file paths are redirected to /app/data to satisfy execution policy.

planned_method.steps:
1. Read Data_Cleaned_22102020.csv from /app/data.
2. Re-create condition variable Cond: 0 = lottery (Lot_check not NA), 1 = gift (Gift_check not NA).
3. Create WTP variable by selecting Lot_WTPc or Gift_WTPc according to Cond; coerce strings like “$20” to numeric.
4. Restrict to participants who passed the comprehension check (check == 3) – CRITICAL TEST.
5. Conduct an independent-samples Welch t-test of mean WTP between Cond groups.
6. Compute Cohen’s d for effect size.
7. (Sensitivity) Repeat test on subset WTP ≤ 20 to match range used in original robustness analysis.
8. Report group Ns, means, t, p, Cohen’s d.
planned_method.models: Independent-samples Welch t-test; Cohen’s d for standardized mean difference.
planned_method.outcome_variable: Willingness to Pay (WTP) in U.S. dollars.

planned_estimation_and_test.estimation: Difference in group means (gift – lottery) and Cohen’s d.

planned_method.missing_data_handling: Listwise deletion: rows with NA or non-numeric WTP, missing Cond, or missing check are removed.
planned_method.inference_criteria: Reject null if p < .05 and the observed mean in lottery < gift (directional confirmation).

files.replicate_analysis.py: End-to-end script that reads the cleaned CSV, reconstructs variables, filters participants, performs Welch t-test and Cohen’s d, prints primary and sensitivity results. Paths hard-coded to /app/data to comply with execution rules.

codebase.notes: The script is a Python translation of the original R file Script.R. No external writes except console output; dependency list kept minimal for fast, reproducible runs.

analysis.instructions: Inside the container (with ./data mounted), run:  python /app/data/replicate_analysis.py
analysis.comparison_metrics: 1) Mean WTP difference (gift – lottery); 2) p-value from Welch t-test; 3) Cohen’s d.  Replication is successful if the sign (lottery < gift) and statistical significance pattern match the original Experiment 1 (initial_details.txt, post_registration.json).
